note: WildHope content. Facts attributed to widely-published sources; verify against primary sources before major decisions. | news[]: hand-curated Good News feed. Each item: {d:"YYYY-MM-DD", tag:"emoji label", t:"title", x:"body", src:"source", url:"optional link"}. Newest first; app shows top 3 on Home. Add items, bump version+updated, push.

categories.name: Sea Turtles
categories.sum: 6 of 7 sea turtle species are threatened; plastic and bycatch are top threats.
categories.overview: Sea turtles have navigated the oceans for over 100 million years, but nearly all species are now threatened. They migrate thousands of kilometres between feeding grounds and the very beaches where they hatched, which makes both their nesting beaches and their ocean routes critical to protect.
categories.science: Family Cheloniidae and Dermochelyidae. A hatchling’s sex is set by nest temperature - warmer sand produces more females, so a warming climate skews sex ratios.
facts: A sea turtle’s sex is decided by the temperature of the sand it incubates in.
Leatherbacks can dive over 1,000 m - deeper than most whales.
Females often return to nest on the same beach where they were born.
threats: Floating bags resemble jellyfish, a favourite food; ingestion and entanglement are widespread.
Accidental capture in nets and longlines is a leading cause of death.
Bright lights disorient hatchlings heading to the sea.
doing: Turtle Excluder Devices (TEDs) let turtles escape trawl nets, cutting bycatch dramatically.
Patrols, hatcheries and “lights-out” rules protect nests and hatchlings.
hope: Nesting numbers rising in places
Decades of beach protection have lifted some green turtle nesting populations.
categories.sci_simple: A baby turtle being a boy or girl is decided by how warm the sand is. Warmer sand makes more girls - so a hotter world means fewer boys.

categories.name: Whales
categories.sum: Many great whales are recovering after the 1986 whaling moratorium.
categories.overview: Whales are ecosystem engineers. Their nutrient-rich waste fertilises tiny phytoplankton that produce oxygen and capture carbon - a process sometimes called the “whale pump.” After the global whaling ban, several populations are recovering, one of conservation’s clearest wins.
categories.science: Order Cetacea. A blue whale is the largest animal ever known to have lived - larger than any dinosaur.
stats: CO₂ a great whale can store
facts: A single great whale can store around 33 tonnes of CO₂ over its lifetime.
Humpbacks sing complex songs that evolve and spread between groups.
Whale waste fertilises the phytoplankton that produce much of our oxygen.
threats: Collisions with vessels injure and kill whales along busy routes.
Discarded and active fishing gear traps whales.
Shipping and sonar disrupt communication and navigation.
doing: Slowing ships in whale zones cuts fatal strikes.
Trained responders free whales caught in gear.
hope: Humpbacks rebound
Some South Atlantic humpback populations have recovered to near pre-whaling numbers.
categories.sci_simple: The blue whale is the biggest animal that has ever lived - even bigger than any dinosaur!

categories.name: Sharks
categories.sum: About a third of sharks and rays face extinction, largely from overfishing.
categories.overview: Sharks are apex predators that keep ocean food webs in balance, yet roughly a third of shark and ray species are threatened. They grow slowly and have few young, so populations recover slowly once overfished.
categories.science: Class Chondrichthyes (cartilaginous fish). Sharks predate the dinosaurs and even trees, having existed for around 450 million years.
facts: Sharks are older than trees - around 450 million years old.
Some sharks must keep swimming to breathe; others can rest on the seabed.
Removing top predators can unbalance entire reef ecosystems.
threats: Demand for fins and slow reproduction drive steep declines.
Sharks are caught unintentionally in fisheries targeting other species.
doing: Nations are closing waters to commercial shark fishing.
Bans on the sale and trade of fins reduce demand.
hope: Palau’s sanctuary
Palau protected its entire exclusive economic zone from commercial shark fishing.
categories.sci_simple: Sharks are super old. They were swimming around about 450 million years ago - before there were dinosaurs, or even trees.

categories.name: Dolphins
categories.sum: Most dolphins are stable, but river dolphins and the vaquita are critical.
categories.overview: Most ocean dolphins are doing relatively well, but several river dolphins and the vaquita porpoise are among the most endangered mammals on Earth. Dolphins are highly social and use signature whistles like names.
categories.science: Family Delphinidae. Dolphins use echolocation - bouncing sound off objects - to hunt and navigate.
facts: Dolphins call each other with individual “signature whistles.”
The vaquita is the world’s most endangered marine mammal.
threats: Gillnets drown dolphins and porpoises like the vaquita.
Chemicals and underwater noise harm coastal dolphins.
doing: Emergency net-free zones aim to save the vaquita.
hope: Protected zones
Dedicated reserves give the rarest porpoises a chance.
categories.sci_simple: Dolphins 'see' with sound. They make clicks that bounce off things, which helps them find food and know where to swim.

categories.name: Elephants
categories.sum: Keystone species that disperse seeds of over 100 tree species.
categories.overview: Elephants shape entire landscapes - knocking down trees, digging waterholes and spreading seeds - which is why they are called a keystone species. African forest elephants are Critically Endangered; savanna elephants are Endangered.
categories.science: Genera Loxodonta (Africa) and Elephas (Asia). Elephants show empathy, mourning and self-recognition in mirrors.
facts: Elephants can recognise themselves in a mirror - a rare sign of self-awareness.
They communicate over kilometres using low rumbles humans can barely hear.
Forest elephants are “gardeners” that keep tropical forests diverse.
threats: Illegal killing for tusks remains a major threat.
Shrinking range brings elephants into conflict with farms.
doing: Local stewardship shares tourism benefits and protects herds.
Rangers and demand-reduction campaigns cut ivory poaching.
hope: Ivory bans working
Major ivory market closures have reduced poaching pressure in places.
categories.sci_simple: Elephants have big feelings. They can feel sad when a friend dies, help each other, and even recognise themselves in a mirror.

categories.name: Gorillas
categories.sum: Mountain gorillas rose above 1,000 - community tourism funded the recovery.
categories.overview: Mountain gorillas are one of conservation’s great comeback stories: careful protection and community-based tourism lifted them above 1,000 individuals. They share about 98% of their DNA with humans.
categories.science: Genus Gorilla. They live in close family groups led by a dominant “silverback” male.
facts: Gorillas share around 98% of their DNA with humans.
Each gorilla has a unique nose print, like a fingerprint.
threats: Human illnesses can devastate gorilla groups.
Farming and mining shrink forest habitat.
doing: Vets track and treat gorilla groups in the wild.
hope: Mountain gorillas passed 1,000
A rare population increase for a great ape.
categories.sci_simple: Gorillas live in families. A big grown-up male with a silver back is the leader who keeps everyone safe.

categories.name: Orangutans
categories.sum: Palm-oil-driven deforestation is the primary threat to their forests.
categories.overview: Orangutans are the only great apes in Asia and spend most of their lives in the trees. All three species are Critically Endangered, mainly because their rainforest is cleared for palm oil and timber.
categories.science: Genus Pongo. Orangutans use tools and pass learned skills between generations - a form of culture.
facts: Orangutans build a fresh sleeping nest in the trees almost every night.
They are among the few animals shown to use tools culturally.
threats: Forest clearing destroys their only habitat.
Infants are taken after mothers are killed.
doing: Demand for RSPO-certified oil reduces forest clearing.
Rescued orangutans are rehabilitated and returned to protected forest.
hope: Protected forest corridors
Reconnecting forest fragments gives orangutans room to move.
categories.sci_simple: Orangutans use tools and teach their babies how to do things - just like people pass down what they know.

categories.name: Tigers
categories.sum: Wild tigers are rising in India, Nepal and Bhutan.
categories.overview: After a century of decline, wild tiger numbers are climbing again in several countries thanks to protected reserves and anti-poaching work - proof that recovery is possible with commitment.
categories.science: Panthera tigris. Every tiger’s stripe pattern is unique, letting researchers identify individuals from camera traps.
facts: No two tigers have the same stripe pattern.
India’s wild tiger population has more than doubled since 2006.
threats: Tigers are killed for skins and body parts.
Fewer prey animals and fragmented forests limit recovery.
doing: Networks of tiger reserves anchor recovery.
Linking reserves lets tigers disperse and breed.
hope: Tigers doubling
India, Nepal and Bhutan report rising wild tiger numbers.
categories.sci_simple: Every tiger has its own stripe pattern, a bit like a fingerprint. No two tigers look exactly the same.

categories.name: Pandas
categories.sum: Downlisted from Endangered to Vulnerable in 2016 - a real success.
categories.overview: The giant panda is a global symbol of conservation. Decades of habitat protection and reserve networks improved its status from Endangered to Vulnerable in 2016.
categories.science: Ailuropoda melanoleuca. Pandas eat bamboo almost exclusively and may spend 12+ hours a day feeding.
facts: A panda can eat over 12 kg of bamboo a day.
Pandas were moved from Endangered to Vulnerable in 2016.
threats: Roads and farming split bamboo forests.
doing: China’s panda reserves protect large areas of habitat.
hope: Downlisted in 2016
A landmark improvement in extinction risk.
categories.sci_simple: Pandas mostly eat bamboo - and they eat SO much that they can spend more than half the day munching.

categories.name: Rhinos
categories.sum: Three of five rhino species are Critically Endangered; poaching is the driver.
categories.overview: Rhinos have roamed for millions of years, but demand for their horn - made of keratin, like fingernails - fuels intense poaching. Intensive protection has stabilised some populations.
categories.science: Family Rhinocerotidae. Rhino horn is keratin and has no proven medicinal value.
facts: Rhino horn is made of keratin - the same protein as human fingernails.
White rhinos recovered from under 100 animals over a century ago.
threats: Illegal demand drives most rhino killing.
doing: Rangers and tracking protect key populations.
Campaigns cut consumer demand for horn.
hope: Southern white rhino comeback
Once near extinction, now the most numerous rhino.
categories.sci_simple: A rhino's horn is made of the same stuff as your fingernails. It can't cure any sickness, even though some people wrongly think it can.

categories.name: Wolves
categories.sum: Apex predators whose return reshapes whole ecosystems.
categories.overview: Wolves are a textbook example of how a top predator can restore balance. Their return to Yellowstone changed deer behaviour, let vegetation recover and even influenced river courses - a “trophic cascade.”
categories.science: Canis lupus. Wolves live in cooperative family packs and communicate by howling.
facts: Returning wolves to Yellowstone helped restore riverbanks and biodiversity.
A wolf pack is usually an extended family led by a breeding pair.
threats: Conflict with livestock drives culling and poisoning.
Development fragments the large ranges wolves need.
doing: Guardian dogs and fladry reduce livestock conflict.
Protected status allows populations to recover.
hope: Yellowstone recovery
Reintroduced wolves triggered a landscape-scale recovery.
categories.sci_simple: Wolves live in family packs and 'talk' to each other by howling.

categories.name: Foxes
categories.sum: Mostly stable; urban coexistence and rodenticide reduction help most.
categories.overview: Foxes are adaptable and mostly stable, thriving even in cities. Their biggest hidden threat is secondary poisoning from rat poisons that move up the food chain.
categories.science: Tribe Vulpini. Red foxes use the Earth’s magnetic field to help judge distance when pouncing.
facts: Foxes may use Earth’s magnetic field to help aim their pounce.
Urban foxes help control rodents naturally.
threats: Poisoned rodents pass toxins to foxes and raptors.
doing: Communities switch to safer pest control.
hope: Safer pest control spreading
More towns are phasing out second-generation rodenticides.
categories.sci_simple: Foxes can sense the Earth a bit like a compass, which helps them judge exactly how far to jump when they pounce.

categories.name: Penguins
categories.sum: Many penguin species decline as fish stocks and sea ice shift.
categories.overview: Penguins are superbly adapted ocean hunters, but many species are declining as warming seas move their prey and reduce the sea ice some depend on. Marine protected areas help rebuild their food supply.
categories.science: Family Spheniscidae. Penguins are flightless birds whose wings evolved into powerful flippers.
facts: Emperor penguins can dive over 500 m and hold their breath for 20 minutes.
Penguin parents often take turns to incubate eggs and feed at sea.
threats: Competition for fish reduces the food penguins need.
Shifting prey and melting ice stress colonies.
doing: No-take zones rebuild the fish penguins rely on.
hope: New protected waters
Fresh MPAs safeguard key penguin feeding grounds.
categories.sci_simple: Penguins are birds that can't fly. Instead, their wings turned into flippers so they can 'fly' through the water.

categories.name: Dogs
categories.sum: Adoption and spay/neuter directly reduce shelter euthanasia.
categories.overview: Millions of dogs enter shelters each year. Adoption, fostering and spay/neuter are the most direct ways to reduce overcrowding and save lives.
categories.science: Canis familiaris. Dogs were the first domesticated animal and read human gestures better than almost any other species.
facts: Dogs can read human pointing and facial expressions unusually well.
Spay/neuter is the single biggest lever on shelter intake.
threats: Too many animals and too few homes lead to euthanasia.
doing: Every adoption frees space for the next animal.
hope: Falling euthanasia rates
Adoption and TNR have cut shelter euthanasia over the years.
categories.sci_simple: Dogs were the very first animal people made friends with. They're amazing at understanding what we point at and how we feel.

categories.name: Cats
categories.sum: TNR humanely stabilizes community cat populations; indoor cats protect birds.
categories.overview: Community (feral) cats are best managed humanely with trap-neuter-return, which stabilises colonies over time. Keeping pet cats indoors or supervised protects both the cats and local birds.
categories.science: Felis catus. A single unspayed pair and their offspring can theoretically produce a large number of cats in a few years.
facts: Trap-neuter-return stabilises community cat colonies humanely.
Keeping cats indoors protects birds and keeps cats safer.
doing: Neutering and returning community cats stabilises numbers.
hope: Healthier colonies
TNR reduces fighting, disease and kitten deaths.
categories.sci_simple: Cats can have lots of kittens very quickly. That's why helping them get spayed or neutered keeps the number of cats healthy.

categories.name: Birds
categories.sum: North America lost ~3 billion birds since 1970; windows and cats are key threats.
categories.overview: Birds are vital pollinators, seed-spreaders and pest controllers, but North America alone has lost around 3 billion birds since 1970. Simple home fixes - bird-safe glass, native plants, keeping cats in - make a real difference.
categories.science: Class Aves. Many songbirds migrate thousands of kilometres, navigating by stars and magnetic fields.
facts: North America has lost roughly 3 billion breeding birds since 1970.
Native plants can double the insect food available to backyard birds.
Some Arctic terns migrate pole to pole each year.
threats: Reflective glass kills large numbers of birds.
Free-roaming cats are a major human-linked cause of bird deaths.
doing: Decals and patterned glass prevent collisions.
Native gardens rebuild insect food webs.
hope: Local rebounds
Native-plant gardens and protections boost some local bird numbers.
categories.sci_simple: Some small birds fly thousands of miles and find their way using the stars and the Earth's magnetic pull.

categories.name: Bees & Pollinators
categories.sum: Pollinators support ~75% of leading food crops.
categories.overview: Pollinators - bees, butterflies, moths, beetles and more - underpin most of the crops we eat and the wild plants that feed other animals. Habitat loss and pesticides are driving declines, but gardens and pesticide-free spaces help fast.
categories.science: Superfamily Apoidea and others. Wild native bees often out-pollinate honeybees per flower visit.
facts: One in three bites of food we eat depends on pollinators.
Most bee species are solitary, not hive-dwellers.
A single native bee patch can support dozens of species.
threats: Neonicotinoids harm bees’ navigation and reproduction.
Monoculture and paving remove forage and nest sites.
doing: Restrictions on harmful pesticides protect pollinators.
hope: Gardens for pollinators
Community pledges are rebuilding pollinator habitat.
categories.sci_simple: Wild bees are great at their job - sometimes they help flowers make fruit even better than honeybees do.

categories.name: Oceans
categories.sum: Oceans absorb ~25% of CO₂; overfishing and plastic are top stressors.
categories.overview: The ocean produces much of the oxygen we breathe, absorbs about a quarter of human CO₂ emissions and around 90% of the extra heat we trap. Protecting it - the goal of “30x30” - protects everything in it.
categories.science: The ocean covers ~71% of Earth’s surface and holds ~97% of its water.
facts: The ocean absorbs roughly a quarter of the CO₂ we emit.
Over 8 million tonnes of plastic enter the ocean each year.
Most of the oxygen we breathe comes from ocean plankton.
threats: Plastic breaks into microplastics that spread through the food web.
Around a third of assessed fish stocks are overfished.
doing: No-take zones let ecosystems and fisheries recover.
A global push to protect 30% of the ocean by 2030.
hope: MPAs expanding
Protected ocean area has grown steadily toward the 30x30 goal.
categories.sci_simple: The ocean is huge. It covers most of the Earth and holds almost all of the world's water.

categories.name: Coral Reefs
categories.sum: Reefs support ~25% of marine species; heat waves cause bleaching.
categories.overview: Coral reefs cover less than 1% of the ocean floor yet support around a quarter of all marine species. Marine heatwaves cause corals to bleach, but reducing local stressors makes reefs more resilient.
categories.science: Corals are animals that live in partnership with algae (zooxanthellae) which give them colour and food; heat stress breaks that partnership.
facts: Reefs support about a quarter of marine species on under 1% of the seafloor.
Coral colour comes from tiny algae living inside the coral.
doing: Nurseries grow and outplant heat-tolerant corals.
Cutting pollution helps reefs survive heat.
hope: Resilient coral found
Heat-tolerant coral strains show promising survival.
categories.sci_simple: Coral is actually a tiny animal that teams up with even tinier algae. The algae give it colour and food - but when the water gets too hot, they split up and the coral turns white.

categories.name: Forests
categories.sum: Forests host ~80% of land biodiversity; deforestation is slowing in places.
categories.overview: Forests store carbon, regulate water and shelter around 80% of land-based species. Agriculture - especially beef, soy and palm oil - drives most deforestation, but certification and rewilding are turning the tide in places.
categories.science: Tropical forests are the most biodiverse habitats on land, with some single trees hosting hundreds of species.
facts: Forests are home to around 80% of the world’s land-based species.
Agriculture drives the majority of tropical deforestation.
threats: Clearing for beef, soy and palm oil is the main driver.
Logging and fire thin and weaken forests.
doing: Native replanting restores habitat and carbon.
hope: Some frontiers slowing
Deforestation rates have fallen in several key regions.
categories.sci_simple: Rainforests are packed with life. A single tree can be home to hundreds of different kinds of creatures.

categories.name: Freshwater
categories.sum: Freshwater species populations fell ~83% since 1970.
categories.overview: Rivers, lakes and wetlands hold a huge share of biodiversity in a tiny share of the world’s water - and freshwater wildlife has declined faster than land or ocean species. Dams, pollution and over-extraction are the main pressures.
categories.science: Freshwater covers under 1% of Earth’s surface but supports roughly 10% of all known species.
facts: Monitored freshwater populations fell around 83% since 1970 - the steepest of any group.
Wetlands filter water and buffer floods for people and wildlife.
threats: Dams block fish migration and alter rivers.
Runoff and over-use degrade rivers and wetlands.
doing: Taking out obsolete dams reconnects rivers.
Restoring wetlands brings back fish and birds.
hope: Rivers reconnecting
Barrier removals are reviving migratory fish in several rivers.
categories.sci_simple: Rivers and lakes are only a tiny slice of the Earth, but a huge number of animals depend on them to live.

categories.name: Farm Animals
categories.sum: Reducing meat and choosing higher-welfare products cut the most suffering.
categories.overview: Tens of billions of animals are farmed each year. For most people, reducing meat consumption and choosing higher-welfare products are among the highest-impact everyday actions for both animal welfare and the environment.
categories.science: Farmed species like pigs and chickens are cognitively complex; pigs solve problems on a level comparable to dogs.
facts: Diet shifts are among the highest-impact everyday climate and welfare actions.
Pigs are highly intelligent and social animals.
threats: Crowded systems limit natural behaviour and welfare.
Livestock is a major driver of land use and emissions.
doing: Cage-free and pasture systems improve welfare.
Reducing meat lowers demand and impact.
hope: Cage-free shift
Many companies have committed to cage-free eggs.
categories.sci_simple: Farm animals are smart. Pigs can solve puzzles about as well as dogs can.

categories.name: Bats
categories.sum: Vital night pollinators and pest-eaters; white-nose syndrome and habitat loss are the big threats.
categories.overview: Bats are the only truly flying mammals and quiet heroes of the night - pollinating plants, spreading seeds, and eating vast numbers of insect pests. A fungal disease and roost loss have hit many species hard, but bat boxes and cave protection are helping.
categories.science: Order Chiroptera, ~1,400 species - a fifth of all mammals. Most insect-eating bats navigate by echolocation, emitting calls and reading the echoes to hunt in total darkness.
categories.sci_simple: Bats "see" in the dark with sound. They make tiny squeaks and listen to the echo bouncing back, which tells them where bugs and branches are.
facts: A single little brown bat can eat over 1,000 mosquito-sized insects in an hour.
Some bats pollinate at night - including the agave used to make tequila.
threats: A cold-loving fungus wakes hibernating bats, burning the fat they need to survive winter.
Sealed caves, felled old trees, and disturbed attics remove safe places to rest.
doing: Putting up roost boxes gives displaced bats a safe home.
Bat-friendly gates keep people out while letting bats fly free.
hope: Cave protection works
Gating key hibernation caves has stabilized some bat colonies.
Farmers value bats
Bats save farmers billions a year in natural pest control, driving new protections.

categories.name: Frogs & Amphibians
categories.sum: The most threatened group of vertebrates on Earth; a deadly fungus and habitat loss drive the crisis.
categories.overview: Frogs, toads, salamanders and newts are living indicators of a healthy planet - their thin skin makes them sensitive to pollution and warming. They are also the most endangered vertebrate class, but captive-breeding and wetland restoration are bringing some back from the brink.
categories.science: Class Amphibia, ~8,700 species. Amphibians breathe partly through their skin, which must stay moist - making them acutely sensitive to pollutants, disease, and drying habitats.
categories.sci_simple: Frogs can breathe through their skin, so their skin has to stay wet. That also means dirty water or pollution hurts them very quickly - they are like an early warning sign for nature.
facts: Amphibians are the most threatened class of vertebrates - over 40% face extinction.
A frog largely drinks and breathes through a patch of skin, not its mouth.
threats: A skin-attacking fungus has devastated frog populations on nearly every continent.
Draining ponds and pesticide runoff destroy breeding sites.
doing: Small clean ponds give frogs safe places to breed.
Zoos raise disease-free frogs to re-seed wild populations.
hope: Species brought back
Captive-bred corroboree frogs and others are being returned to the wild.
Fungus-resistant frogs
Some populations are evolving resistance to chytrid and recovering.

categories.name: Butterflies & Moths
categories.sum: Beautiful pollinators in steep decline; native plants and pesticide-free gardens bring them back fast.
categories.overview: Butterflies and moths pollinate wildflowers and feed countless birds - and their caterpillars are the base of many food webs. Populations have dropped sharply, but planting native host plants and leaving wild patches produces visible results within a single season.
categories.science: Order Lepidoptera, ~180,000 species. Most caterpillars are specialists - monarchs, for example, can only develop on milkweed, so losing one plant can mean losing the butterfly.
categories.sci_simple: Baby butterflies (caterpillars) are picky eaters. Monarchs, for example, can only eat milkweed leaves - so if the milkweed disappears, the butterflies do too.
facts: Monarch butterflies migrate up to 3,000 miles, a journey spanning multiple generations.
Moths outnumber butterflies roughly 9 to 1 and do much of the night-time pollinating.
threats: Tidy lawns and herbicides wipe out the milkweed and wildflowers caterpillars need.
Insecticides kill caterpillars and the adults that feed on treated flowers.
doing: Milkweed and local wildflowers feed caterpillars and adults.
Un-mowed corners give eggs and chrysalises a place to survive.
hope: Monarch highways
Roadside and garden milkweed corridors are being planted across North America.
Gardens fill up fast
A patch of native flowers can draw butterflies back within weeks.

categories.name: Wetlands
categories.sum: Earth’s most valuable ecosystem per acre - and among the fastest disappearing.
categories.overview: Marshes, swamps, bogs and mangroves store carbon, filter water, buffer floods, and shelter an astonishing share of wildlife. We have lost a third of the world’s wetlands since 1970, but restoration is one of the highest-return actions in conservation.
categories.science: Wetlands cover ~6% of land but support ~40% of all species. Peat wetlands alone store twice as much carbon as all the world’s forests combined.
categories.sci_simple: Wetlands are like the planet’s sponges and filters. They soak up floods, clean dirty water, and lock away huge amounts of carbon that would otherwise warm the Earth.
facts: Wetlands are disappearing three times faster than forests.
Coastal mangroves can absorb wave energy and blunt the force of storm surges.
threats: Wetlands are filled for farmland and building, releasing stored carbon.
Fertilizer and plastic runoff choke the water and its life.
doing: Re-flooding drained land quickly revives wetland life and carbon storage.
Home rain gardens mimic wetlands, filtering runoff before it pollutes.
hope: Rewetting works fast
Restored marshes attract birds and store carbon within a few years.
Mangrove revival
Community mangrove planting is protecting coastlines and nurseries for fish.

refuse-plastic.why: 8M+ tonnes of plastic enter the ocean yearly, harming turtles, seabirds and whales.

meatless-meal.why: Animal agriculture is a top driver of land use, emissions and farmed-animal suffering.
meatless-meal.imp: Saves ~1-2 kg CO₂e vs a beef meal.

switch-palm-oil.why: Unsustainable palm oil drives tropical deforestation that destroys orangutan, tiger and elephant habitat.
switch-palm-oil.imp: Pressures supply chains toward certified sources.

beach-cleanup.why: Coastal litter is ingested by marine life; small cleanups add up across communities.

native-plant.why: Native plants feed local pollinators and birds far better than ornamentals.
native-plant.imp: Can support dozens of bee & butterfly species.
steps: Find a native species for your region

bee-hotel.why: Solitary bees need nesting cavities lost to development.
steps: Buy or build with untreated wood

bird-feeder.t: Set up a bird feeder & water
bird-feeder.why: Clean feeders and window decals help birds and prevent collisions.

sustainable-seafood.why: Overfishing and bycatch threaten sharks, turtles and fish stocks.

cats-indoors.why: Free-roaming cats are a leading human-linked cause of bird deaths.
cats-indoors.imp: Protects local birds - and keeps cats safer.
steps: Try a catio or leash walks

reduce-food-waste.t: Save one meal from the bin
reduce-food-waste.why: Food waste wastes the land, water and animal lives used to produce it.
reduce-food-waste.imp: Cuts emissions and demand on farming.

save-water.why: Freshwater habitats and their species are among the most threatened on Earth.
save-water.imp: Less demand on rivers and wetlands.
steps: Turn off the tap while scrubbing

adopt.t: Adopt or foster from a shelter
adopt.why: Shelters are overcrowded; adoption and fostering save lives and free space.
adopt.imp: Each adoption can save two animals.

spay-neuter.why: The most effective way to reduce shelter intake and euthanasia.
spay-neuter.imp: One prevented litter averts dozens of shelter animals.
steps: Book a clinic or sponsor one

donate.why: Effective orgs turn funds into ranger patrols, habitat and rescue capacity.
donate.imp: Small recurring gifts fund real anti-poaching work.

sign-petition.why: Public pressure shifts policy on protected areas and trade bans.
sign-petition.imp: Has preceded real bans and marine reserves.

reduce-pesticides.why: Pesticides harm pollinators and cause secondary poisoning up the food chain.
reduce-pesticides.imp: Your yard becomes a refuge for bees & birds.

plant-tree.t: Plant or fund a native tree
plant-tree.why: Reforestation restores habitat and captures carbon with native species.
plant-tree.imp: One native tree supports hundreds of interactions.
steps: Plant or fund via a vetted program

educate-child.t: Teach a child one animal fact
educate-child.why: Early nature connection is the strongest predictor of lifelong conservation behavior.
educate-child.imp: One fact plants a multi-decade ripple of care.
steps: Pick a fact from the app

report-abuse.t: Learn how to report animal abuse
report-abuse.why: Fast, correct reporting gets animals help and triggers enforcement.
report-abuse.imp: The right hotline can save a life in minutes.

compost-scraps.why: Food in landfill emits methane; compost returns nutrients to soil and cuts waste that drives habitat-clearing agriculture.
compost-scraps.imp: Diverts ~150 kg of waste from landfill a year.
steps: Add greens + browns, keep it moist
Use the compost in your garden

brake-for-wildlife.t: Drive slower on rural roads at dusk
brake-for-wildlife.why: Millions of wild animals die in vehicle collisions yearly; dawn and dusk are peak crossing times.
brake-for-wildlife.imp: Prevents road deaths of deer, foxes, owls and more.
steps: Ease off at dawn/dusk near woods & water

wildlife-window.why: Up to a billion birds a year die striking glass they can't see.
wildlife-window.imp: Stops repeat collisions at your home.
steps: Move feeders very close to or far from glass

secondhand-first.t: Buy one thing secondhand instead of new
secondhand-first.why: Making new goods drives mining, logging and emissions that shrink wild habitat.
secondhand-first.imp: Cuts demand on raw materials and land.
steps: Check a charity shop or resale app first

no-mow.t: Leave part of your lawn unmown
no-mow.why: Wildflower patches feed pollinators and shelter insects that birds depend on.
no-mow.imp: Turns dead lawn into living habitat.
steps: Pick a corner to let grow

ditch-microbeads.why: Microbeads and synthetic glitter wash into waterways and enter the food chain.
ditch-microbeads.imp: Keeps plastics out of rivers and oceans.

rain-garden.why: Clean water and healthy runoff support amphibians, birds and insects, and ease pressure on rivers.
rain-garden.imp: Creates a water source and filters runoff.
steps: Place a shallow dish or barrel
Add stones for insects to land on

citizen-science.t: Log a wildlife sighting for science
citizen-science.why: Your observations help scientists track species, ranges and declines in real time.
citizen-science.imp: Adds a data point real researchers use.
steps: Photograph or note what you see

shade-coffee.why: Shade coffee keeps forest canopy standing - vital habitat for migratory birds.

wildlife-corridor.t: Make a gap for wildlife in your fence
wildlife-corridor.why: Hedgehogs, frogs and small mammals need to roam between gardens to find food and mates.
wildlife-corridor.imp: Reconnects fragmented habitat block by block.
steps: Cut a 13cm gap at fence base
Ask neighbours to do the same

walk-dont-drive.t: Walk or bike one short trip
walk-dont-drive.why: Transport emissions drive the warming that bleaches reefs and shifts habitats.
walk-dont-drive.imp: ~0.5 kg CO₂e saved per short car trip skipped.

green-energy.t: Switch to a green energy plan
green-energy.why: Home electricity is one of your biggest footprints - one switch changes it every day after.
green-energy.imp: Can cut ~1,000+ kg CO₂e per year.

nest-box.why: Modern buildings lack the cavities swifts, tits and sparrows nest in.
nest-box.imp: Homes a breeding pair every spring.
steps: Buy or build a species-right box
Mount high, away from feeders & sun

plant-milk.t: Try a plant milk in one drink
plant-milk.why: Dairy has roughly 3× the emissions and far more land use than plant milks.

cold-wash.why: Cold, full loads cut energy and shed fewer microplastics into rivers and seas.
cold-wash.imp: Saves energy and keeps microfibres out of waterways.

fashion-detox.t: Buy no new clothes this month
fashion-detox.why: Fashion is a top polluter of waterways and a major carbon source.
fashion-detox.imp: Skipping one new garment saves ~10+ kg CO₂e.

local-seasonal.why: Seasonal local produce needs less energy, transport and packaging.
local-seasonal.imp: Lower footprint and supports local growers.

write-representative.why: Habitat and wildlife protections are decided in policy - constituent letters get counted.
write-representative.imp: Real signal that voters care about nature.

volunteer-hour.why: Shelters, rescues and habitat groups run on volunteer time.
volunteer-hour.imp: One hour = walked dogs, cleaned habitats, planted natives.
steps: Find a local shelter or nature group

tree-search.why: Some search engines fund reforestation with their ad revenue - searching becomes habitat.

courses.d: How plastic and pollution affect marine life - and what actually works.

lessons.body: Most ocean plastic starts on land. Litter and poorly managed waste wash down drains and rivers into the sea, where sunlight and waves break it into ever-smaller microplastics. Because it fragments rather than disappears, plastic keeps circulating through the food web for decades.
lessons.body_simple: Most plastic in the sea starts on land. Trash washes down drains and rivers into the ocean, where it breaks into tiny bits called microplastics that stick around for a very long time.

lessons.body: Floating plastic bags look like jellyfish to a hungry sea turtle, and seabirds mistake fragments for food. Ingestion blocks stomachs, while discarded nets - “ghost gear” - entangle whales, seals and turtles. Microplastics have now been found from the deepest trenches to Arctic ice.

quiz.q: A common plastic harm to sea turtles is…

lessons.body_simple: A floating plastic bag looks like a tasty jellyfish to a turtle, and birds think small bits are food. Eating plastic makes animals sick, and old fishing nets can trap whales and seals.

lessons.body: The most effective step is reduction - avoiding single-use plastic in the first place - followed by reuse, cleanups and policy like bag bans and deposit schemes. Individual swaps matter, but they matter most when they build momentum for system-wide change.
lessons.body_simple: The best thing to do is use less plastic in the first place. Reusing things, cleaning up beaches, and rules like bag bans all help too.

courses.d: How extinction risk is measured - and how it gets reversed.

lessons.body: The IUCN Red List ranks species from Least Concern (LC) up through Near Threatened (NT), Vulnerable (VU), Endangered (EN) and Critically Endangered (CR), to Extinct in the Wild (EW) and Extinct (EX). The category reflects how close a species is to disappearing, based on population size, trend and range.
lessons.body_simple: Scientists sort animals by how much danger they're in - from 'doing fine' all the way to 'almost gone.' It's based on how many are left and whether their numbers are dropping.

lessons.body: The biggest drivers are habitat loss, over-exploitation (hunting, fishing, trade), invasive species, pollution and climate change - often several at once. Because these pressures are human-caused, they can also be human-reduced.

quiz.q: A leading cause of species decline is…

lessons.body_simple: Animals get in trouble mostly because of people: losing their homes, being hunted too much, pollution, and a changing climate. The good news is that people can fix these things too.

lessons.body: Pandas moved from Endangered to Vulnerable, tigers are rising in Asia, and humpback whales rebounded after whaling bans. Habitat protection, corridors, anti-poaching and vetted funding are the tools that turn decline into recovery.
lessons.body_simple: It works! Pandas, tigers, and humpback whales are all doing better now because people protected their homes and stopped the hunting.

courses.d: Palm oil, seafood and everyday choices that protect wildlife.

lessons.body: Certifications help you choose products that do less harm: RSPO for sustainable palm oil, MSC or a seafood guide for fish, and cruelty-free marks for cosmetics. Labels aren’t perfect, but they steer demand toward better practices.

quiz.q: Certified sustainable palm oil is labelled…

lessons.body_simple: Special labels help you pick kinder products - like ones that don't hurt forests or animals. Labels aren't perfect, but they push companies to do better.

lessons.body: Not all choices are equal. Cutting back on beef, choosing certified seafood and avoiding uncertified palm oil deliver far more benefit per effort than switching small items. Focus energy where the footprint is biggest.
lessons.body_simple: Some choices help way more than others. Eating less beef and picking good seafood matter a lot more than tiny swaps. Put your effort where it counts most.

lessons.body: “Eco” and “natural” have no fixed meaning. Look for specific, verifiable claims and independent certification rather than vague marketing. When in doubt, favour less consumption over a “greener” version of more.
lessons.body_simple: Words like 'eco' and 'natural' don't really mean much on their own. Look for clear proof, and remember: buying less is often better than buying a 'greener' version of more.

courses.d: Why variety of life keeps ecosystems - and us - healthy.

lessons.body: Biodiversity is the variety of life at three levels: genes within a species, the species themselves, and whole ecosystems. More variety generally means more resilience - a system that can absorb shocks like disease or drought.
lessons.body_simple: Biodiversity means lots of different kinds of life. The more variety there is, the stronger nature is at handling problems like disease or drought.

lessons.body: Biodiversity provides food, medicine, clean water, pollination, flood protection and climate stability. These “ecosystem services” are worth trillions and are difficult or impossible to replace with technology.
lessons.body_simple: Nature gives us food, medicine, clean water, and helps flowers grow. These gifts are worth a fortune and are really hard to replace.

lessons.body: You don’t need a rainforest to help. Native plants, pesticide-free gardens, keeping cats indoors and supporting local habitat corridors all rebuild biodiversity where you live.

quiz.q: A local win for biodiversity is…

lessons.body_simple: You can help right where you live - plant native flowers, skip the bug spray, keep cats inside, and support green spaces near you.

courses.d: Marine protected areas, sustainable fishing and the 30x30 goal.

lessons.body: Marine protected areas (MPAs) set aside parts of the ocean where harmful activity is limited. Well-enforced “no-take” zones let fish grow larger and more numerous, and the surplus spills over into surrounding waters - helping both wildlife and fishers.

quiz.q: A no-take MPA mainly helps by…
opts: Letting fish recover and spill over

lessons.body_simple: Some parts of the ocean are set aside as safe zones where fishing is limited. Fish there grow bigger and have more babies, and some swim out to fill nearby waters too.

lessons.body: Overfishing and bycatch are the ocean’s biggest wildlife pressures. Better gear (like turtle-excluder devices), catch limits and certification reduce harm while keeping fisheries viable for the long term.
lessons.body_simple: Catching too many fish is the ocean's biggest problem. Smarter nets and catch limits let us still get food without harming as much wildlife.

lessons.body: Countries have agreed to protect 30% of land and sea by 2030 - “30x30.” It’s ambitious: ocean protection is rising but still short of the target, so public support and new MPAs matter.
lessons.body_simple: Countries promised to protect 3 out of every 10 parts of the sea and land by the year 2030. We're getting there, but not fast enough - so speaking up helps.

courses.d: Why it happens - and how demand reduction stops it.

lessons.body: Illegal wildlife trade is driven by demand for products like ivory, rhino horn, exotic pets and traditional remedies. It is one of the largest illegal trades in the world and pushes species like rhinos toward extinction.

quiz.q: The trade is driven mainly by…

lessons.body_simple: Some people illegally buy and sell animal parts like ivory and horns, or wild animals as pets. This is a huge problem that can push animals toward disappearing forever.

lessons.body: Rangers, sniffer dogs, detection technology and international agreements like CITES intercept trafficking. But enforcement alone can’t win - cutting demand is essential.
lessons.body_simple: Rangers, sniffer dogs, and special laws help stop this trade. But the real fix is getting people to stop wanting to buy these things.

lessons.body: Never buy wildlife products - ivory, tortoiseshell, coral, exotic pets or “medicinal” animal parts. Support demand-reduction campaigns and report suspected trafficking. Buyers, not just poachers, drive the trade.
lessons.body_simple: Never buy things made from wild animals. If shoppers stop buying, the hunters stop hunting.

courses.d: Coexisting with the wild neighbours in our towns and gardens.

lessons.body: Towns aren't wildlife deserts. Gardens, parks and green roofs together form huge habitat networks - often richer than the intensive farmland around them. Foxes, hedgehogs, bats, swifts and countless pollinators live alongside us. The key is connection: many small friendly spaces linked together beat one big isolated one.

quiz.q: What makes urban areas valuable for wildlife?

lessons.body_simple: Cities aren't empty of wildlife. Gardens, parks, and green roofs join up to make homes for foxes, bats, birds, and bees. Lots of small friendly spaces linked together work best.

lessons.body: Most human-wildlife conflict comes from food and gaps. Secure bins, don't leave pet food out, and block access to roof and chimney cavities humanely - before, not after, animals move in. Never poison: rodenticides travel up the food chain and kill the owls and foxes that control pests for free. Tolerance and simple prevention solve almost everything.

opts: They poison predators up the food chain

lessons.body_simple: Most trouble with wild animals is about food and gaps. Keep bins closed, don't leave pet food out, and block off holes kindly before animals move in. Never use poison - it travels up and kills the owls and foxes that eat pests for free.

lessons.body: Small acts compound: a fence gap for hedgehogs, a pesticide-free patch, a shallow water dish, native plants for pollinators, and bird-safe windows. Log what you see with citizen-science apps so the animals you help also become data that protects others.

quiz.q: A simple way to help urban wildlife is…
opts: A gap in the fence and a water dish

lessons.body_simple: Little things add up: a gap in the fence for hedgehogs, a bug-spray-free patch, a dish of water, native flowers, and stickers so birds don't hit windows.

facts: A single great whale can store around 33 tonnes of CO₂ over its lifetime.
Elephants can recognise themselves in a mirror - a rare marker of self-awareness.
One in three bites of food we eat depends on pollinators.
A sea turtle’s sex is set by nest temperature - warmer sand yields more females.
Sharks have existed for around 450 million years - older than trees.
Octopuses have three hearts and blue, copper-based blood.
Coral reefs cover under 1% of the ocean floor but support ~25% of marine species.
India’s wild tiger population has more than doubled since 2006.
Returning wolves to Yellowstone helped restore riverbanks and biodiversity.
North America has lost roughly 3 billion breeding birds since 1970.
The ocean produces much of the oxygen we breathe, via tiny plankton.
Gorillas share around 98% of their DNA with humans.
A single oak tree can support over 2,000 species of birds, insects, fungi and mammals.
Hedgehogs can travel up to 2 km in a single night looking for food and mates.
Bats eat thousands of insects an hour - natural, free pest control for farms and gardens.
Beavers are ecosystem engineers: their dams create wetlands that boost biodiversity and reduce flooding.
Swifts can stay airborne for up to 10 months without landing - eating and even sleeping on the wing.
stories: Giant pandas leave the Endangered list
Decades of habitat protection moved pandas to Vulnerable in 2016.
Coordinated reserves and anti-poaching drove the recovery since 2006.
Hundreds cleaned, rehabilitated and returned to the wild by a coastal centre.
Palau creates a vast shark sanctuary
Palau banned commercial shark fishing across its entire EEZ.
Community-based tourism funded a real population rebound.
Removing obsolete dams is reviving migratory fish across several rivers.

news.t: The Iberian lynx keeps bouncing back
news.x: From about 100 animals in 2002 to thousands today - downlisted from Endangered to Vulnerable. Coordinated breeding and rabbit recovery did it.
Several Southern Hemisphere populations have largely recovered since the 1986 whaling moratorium - proof that protection works at ocean scale.
From just 22 birds in the 1980s to over 500 today, more than half flying free. One of the great rescue stories in conservation.
Once extinct in the wild, thousands now graze rewilded landscapes across Europe - restoring meadows and biodiversity as they go.

news.t: Momentum builds for protecting the high seas
news.x: Nations continue ratifying the ocean treaty aimed at protecting 30% of international waters - habitats sharks, whales and turtles depend on.
